question: Explain the operation of the Emergency Core Cooling System (ECCS) during a Loss of Coolant Accident (LOCA).

nuscale_ch05_0023.title: Reactor Coolant System High-Point Vents
nuscale_ch05_0023.summary: This section outlines the testing and inspection requirements for the DHRS (Decay Heat Removal System) components in the NuScale reactor, ensuring compliance with ASME codes for Class 2 components. It specifies that DHRS actuation valves are exercised during cold shutdown conditions, and that comprehensive testing—including position verification and in-situ heat removal tests—will be performed according to technical specifications and as part of the initial test program for the first reactor module.

nuscale_ch05_0004.title: System Description
nuscale_ch05_0004.summary: The Reactor Coolant System (RCS) in the NuScale design is located within the containment vessel and is responsible for transferring heat from the reactor core to the steam generators using natural circulation. It includes components such as the reactor pressure vessel, pressurizer, and various valves, and features systems for removing noncondensible gases and maintaining the integrity of the pressure boundary to prevent radionuclide release. The RCS interfaces with several other systems, including emergency core cooling and chemical control, to ensure safe and efficient operation.

nuscale_ch05_0003.title: Design Basis
nuscale_ch05_0003.summary: The Design Basis section outlines that the NuScale reactor coolant system (RCS) uses natural circulation without pumps or external piping, ensuring safe and efficient heat transfer from the reactor core to the steam generator. The RCS is designed to maintain proper coolant flow, water chemistry, and boron concentration to protect fuel integrity and support safe operation under normal and transient conditions.

nuscale_ch05_0006.title: System Evaluation
nuscale_ch05_0006.summary: The System Evaluation section describes how the NuScale Power Plant optimizes performance across different power levels by establishing steady-state parameters for both primary and secondary systems, such as coolant temperatures and flow rates. These parameters are calculated using thermal hydraulic software and verified by hand, with uncertainties and design margins considered to ensure efficient operation and safety. The design supports natural circulation with low pressure losses, and uncertainties related to steam generator tube plugging do not affect primary coolant flow rate determination.

nuscale_ch05_0025.title: References
nuscale_ch05_0025.summary: This section details the materials used in the construction of the NuScale pressurizer and its components, specifying alloys and standards for each part to ensure corrosion resistance and compatibility with operating environments. It references relevant sections of the BPVC (Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code) and lists the specific alloys and material specifications for the baffle plate, heater bundles, cover plate, bolting, and heater sheaths.

nuscale_ch05_0012.title: Reactor Coolant Pressure Boundary Leakage Detection
nuscale_ch05_0012.summary: The NuScale reactor coolant system (RCS) does not use traditional components with designed leakage rates, making it impractical to distinguish between identified and unidentified leakage inside containment using current instrumentation. All leakage into containment is treated as unidentified until the source is determined, with leakage rates monitored by RCS inventory balance and containment evacuation system (CES) flow rates, and operational limits set in technical specifications. The leakage detection systems are designed to be reliable, redundant, and sensitive enough to support leak-before-break analyses.

nuscale_ch05_0022.title: Decay Heat Removal System
nuscale_ch05_0022.summary: The Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS) is an engineered safety feature designed to remove residual and core decay heat from the reactor after shutdown, especially when normal cooling is unavailable, without relying on external power. It ensures the reactor coolant system temperature drops below 420°F within 36 hours, maintaining fuel and pressure boundary safety limits, and its reliability and risk significance are evaluated through established programs. The DHRS operates independently of the Emergency Core Cooling System (ECCS), but both can function together if needed.

edges.description: Section B explains that reactor vent valves (RVVs) are opened as part of ECCS operation to remove noncondensible gases during emergencies, while Section A details the inspection and classification requirements for components including actuation valves, which would encompass the RVVs referenced in Section B.

Section A details inspection and classification requirements for components (such as actuation valves) that are part of the systems supporting the reactor coolant system described in Section B, thereby specifying implementation details for the general system overview in Section B.

Section A specifies inspection and classification requirements for DHRS components, which are part of the system described in Section B as being connected to the steam and feed piping to remove decay heat during events like a LOCA.

Section A provides specific operational details of the ECCS during emergency conditions, elaborating on components described in the general system overview of Section B, thereby specifying how the RCS and its subsystems function during a LOCA.

Section A describes specific ECCS components and their function during emergency core cooling, while Section B outlines the evaluation criteria for system performance, which would include assessing the effectiveness of ECCS operation as described in Section A.

Section A describes the operational function of the reactor vent valves (RVVs) in the ECCS during a LOCA, while Section B provides detailed material specifications for pressurizer components that are referenced in Section A's system description.

Section A describes the design basis and configuration of the reactor coolant system, which is foundational for the system evaluation in Section B that assesses performance criteria and operational parameters based on that design.

Section A provides a general overview of the reactor coolant system, including the integral pressurizer, while Section B specifies detailed design and material information about the pressurizer components mentioned in Section A.
Section A references Section B by directing the reader to Section 5.4 for further description of the steam generators, which is the subject of Section B.

Section A discusses the challenges in detecting and distinguishing reactor coolant pressure boundary leakage, while Section B specifies inspection and testing requirements for components (including valves) that could be sources of such leakage, connecting them through the need to ensure integrity and operability of systems relevant to ECCS operation during a LOCA.

Section B describes how the ECCS uses reactor vent valves to discharge steam and remove noncondensible gases during emergency core cooling operation, while Section A discusses the challenges in detecting RCS boundary leakage, which is relevant to understanding ECCS operation during a LOCA because leakage detection affects ECCS initiation and performance.

Section A discusses the challenges in detecting and distinguishing leakage within the reactor coolant pressure boundary due to the unique design of the NuScale RCS, while Section B describes that the RCS design omits traditional components like pumps and external piping, which directly explains the context for the leakage detection limitations in Section A.
Section B specifies the inspection and classification requirements for DHRS components, which are generally described in Section A as part of the Decay Heat Removal System's design and function.

Section A describes the Decay Heat Removal System's role in non-LOCA events, while Section B provides a general overview of the Reactor Coolant System, with both sections connected by their discussion of systems responsible for heat removal and coolant circulation in the reactor.

Section A discusses the detection of reactor coolant pressure boundary leakage, which is a precursor to a LOCA event that would require activation of the ECCS, while Section B describes the Decay Heat Removal System, which is designed for non-LOCA events and thus is not the primary system for ECCS operation during a LOCA.
